items.q: Is a hard material always a tough material?
items.correct: No - hardness and toughness are different properties; glass is hard but brittle, so it shatters under impact.
items.misconception: Yes - if a material is hard it must also be tough.
items.random_wrong: Yes - hardness and toughness are two names for the same property.
items.why: Students treat hard and tough as synonyms. Hardness resists surface scratching and wear; toughness resists impact. Many hard materials are brittle.

items.q: Are all hardwoods physically harder than all softwoods?
items.correct: No - the terms describe the tree type, broadleaved deciduous versus coniferous; balsa is a hardwood yet very soft.
items.misconception: Yes - hardwood means the wood is always harder than softwood.
items.random_wrong: Hardwood means wood that has been painted or treated.
items.why: The botanical naming misleads students. Hardwood/softwood classifies the tree, not the measured hardness, and exceptions like soft balsa prove the point.

items.q: Is density a working property of a material?
items.correct: No - density is a physical property; working properties describe how a material responds to forces, such as strength or toughness.
items.misconception: Yes - every measurable property of a material is a working property.
items.random_wrong: Density is an aesthetic property because it affects appearance.
items.why: Students lump all properties together. Physical properties exist without loading (density, conductivity); working properties only show when the material is worked or stressed.

items.q: A material that resists scratching and wear is described as:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Hard. “Tough” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: The ability to absorb impact energy without fracturing
items.why: It's The ability to absorb impact energy without fracturing. “Resistance to scratching” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which of these is a physical property rather than a working property?
items.why: It's Thermal conductivity. “Tensile strength” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Drawn out into thin wire without breaking
items.why: Correct answer: Drawn out into thin wire without breaking. “Shattered easily by impact” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A product uses 0.4 kg of plastic per unit. How many kilograms of plastic are needed for 250 units?
items.why: Exact answer: 100 kg. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
